questions.question: Why can a shared cache cluster become an incident amplifier instead of only a performance booster?
options: Because cache nodes are unable to store values larger than one kilobyte.
Because node loss or cold rebalancing can turn many cheap hits into simultaneous expensive origin misses.
Because caches cannot support TTL on stored objects.
Because every cache automatically persists all values forever.
questions.explanation: When many misses happen together, the origin may take the full blast.

questions.question: What is the main reason to use consistent hashing in a cache cluster?
options: It reduces key movement when nodes are added or removed.
It makes all cache entries strongly consistent.
It removes the need for eviction policies.
It guarantees perfect balance even under any skew.
questions.explanation: Consistent hashing is primarily about reducing churn during topology changes.

questions.question: What problem does request coalescing solve most directly?
options: It guarantees every cache key fits in memory.
It collapses many concurrent misses for the same key into one origin fetch.
It permanently eliminates stale reads.
It prevents any node from ever failing.
questions.explanation: Coalescing protects the origin from duplicate work during popular misses.

questions.question: Which signal best reveals that one tenant is polluting the cache?
options: High eviction churn paired with poor hit rate for that tenant or object class.
The number of code comments in the cache service.
Average TLS handshake size.
Only total cluster memory allocated.
questions.explanation: Pollution shows up as churn without proportional benefit.

questions.question: What is the trade-off of keeping very short TTLs on cached objects?
options: Freshness improves, but origin pressure rises because entries expire more often.
Memory usage becomes zero for hot keys.
Node failures no longer matter.
Eviction policies are no longer required.
questions.explanation: Freshness is bought with more frequent repopulation.

questions.question: Why might a team choose segmented cache pools instead of one shared pool?
options: To improve tenant isolation or separate object classes with very different behavior.
Because consistent hashing only works with exactly two nodes.
To disable negative caching globally.
Because shared caches cannot expose metrics.
questions.explanation: Segmentation reduces noisy-neighbor effects at the cost of aggregate efficiency.

questions.question: Which observability detail is most helpful after a sharp hit-rate drop during scaling?
options: Key movement and hit-rate regression by shard after the topology change.
Only the number of engineers on call.
The average length of cache keys.
Daily repository commit count.
questions.explanation: You want to know whether the scale event itself caused cold shards and churn.

questions.question: Why can negative caching be useful but dangerous?
options: It reduces repeated lookups for missing data, but stale not-found entries can hide newly created objects.
It improves durability for irreplaceable data.
It makes cache clusters immune to hot keys.
It removes the need for version tokens.
questions.explanation: Negative caching is great for load reduction, but it can serve stale absence.
